subculture was an early-1970s offshoot of [skinhead](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead") subculture in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"). Although sharing similarities to 1960s skinheads, suedeheads grew their hair longer and dressed more formally.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Suedeheads-2) Although often [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") like skinheads, some had [white collar](/wiki/White-collar_worker "White-collar worker") jobs. A female suedehead was a *sort*.

["Britain: The Skinheads"](https://web.archive.org/web/20070930193315/http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,909318,00.html?promoid=googlep). *Time*. 1970-06-08. Archived from [the original](http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,909318,00.html?promoid=googlep) on September 30, 2007. Retrieved 2010-05-24.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Early-1970s offshoot of skinhead subculture in the United Kingdom and Ireland

Suedehead

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Illustration of a male suedehead leaning on the [Trojan Records](/wiki/Trojan_Records "Trojan Records") logo | |
| Years active | 1970s-1980s |
| Country | [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") |
| Major figures | [Hoxton Tom McCourt](/wiki/Hoxton_Tom_McCourt "Hoxton Tom McCourt") |
| Influences | [Skinhead](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead"), [Mod](/wiki/Mod_(subculture) "Mod (subculture)") |
| Influenced | [Mod revival](/wiki/Mod_revival "Mod revival") |

The **suedehead** subculture was an early-1970s offshoot of [skinhead](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead") subculture in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"). Although sharing similarities to 1960s skinheads, suedeheads grew their hair longer and dressed more formally.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Suedeheads-2) Although often [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") like skinheads, some had [white collar](/wiki/White-collar_worker "White-collar worker") jobs. A female suedehead was a *sort*.

Suedeheads wore [brogues](/wiki/Brogue_shoe "Brogue shoe"), [loafers](/wiki/Slip-on_shoe "Slip-on shoe") or [basketweave](/wiki/Basketweave "Basketweave") [Norwegians](/wiki/Slip-on_shoe "Slip-on shoe") instead of heavy boots. Suedeheads wore [suits](/wiki/Suit_(clothing) "Suit (clothing)") (especially in check patterns such as [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Glen_plaid "Glen plaid") and [dogtooth](/wiki/Houndstooth "Houndstooth")) and other dressy outfits as everyday wear instead of just at dancehalls. [Crombie](/wiki/Crombie_(clothing) "Crombie (clothing)")-style overcoats and [sheepskin](/wiki/Sheepskin "Sheepskin") coats became common. Most London suedeheads wore a silk handkerchief in the chest pocket of their Crombie, which also had a circular tie-pin through the Crombie and the handkerchief. Shirts often had large button-down collars, usually either pointed or rounded, called *butterfly collars*. The top shirts were [Ben Shermans](/wiki/Ben_Sherman "Ben Sherman") with a back pleat and top loop. Early on the most common style was a large windowpane check worn under a [tank top](/wiki/Tank_top_(sweater) "Tank top (sweater)"). At the height of the era, shirts changed to muted pastel shades, with the colour being governed by the day of the week. Sta-Prest [trousers](/wiki/Trousers "Trousers") became worn more than jeans, which had been common with skinheads. Although the most popular form of trousers were the 2Tone [Tonik](/w/index.php?title=Tonik_(fabric)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tonik (fabric) (page does not exist)"), which changed colour as they moved. The most common base colours were blue and green, whilst the most favoured secondary colours were red, yellow and gold. Another characteristic was coloured socks—such as solid red or blue—instead of plain black or white.[[2]](#cite_note-Suedeheads-2)

Suedeheads shared the skinheads' interest in [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady"), [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), [R&B](/wiki/Rhythm_and_Blues "Rhythm and Blues"), [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk") and [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"),[[2]](#cite_note-Suedeheads-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Morrissey_Gets_It_Off_His_Chest-3) but some suedeheads also listened to [British](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") [glam rock](/wiki/Glam_rock "Glam rock") bands such as [The Sweet](/wiki/The_Sweet "The Sweet"), [Slade](/wiki/Slade "Slade"), [Ziggy Stardust](/wiki/Ziggy_Stardust_(character) "Ziggy Stardust (character)") and [Mott the Hoople](/wiki/Mott_the_Hoople "Mott the Hoople").[[2]](#cite_note-Suedeheads-2)[[4]](#cite_note-4) In the late 1970s, most Suedeheads closely followed groups such as [The Beat](/wiki/The_Beat_(British_band) "The Beat (British band)"), [Madness](/wiki/Madness_(band) "Madness (band)"), [the Specials](/wiki/The_Specials "The Specials") and other artists on the [2Tone](/wiki/2Tone_music "2Tone music") [record label](/wiki/2Tone_records "2Tone records").

Suedeheads were portrayed in the [east end London](/wiki/East_End_of_London "East End of London")-based film *[Bronco Bullfrog](/wiki/Bronco_Bullfrog "Bronco Bullfrog")* and the [Richard Allen](/wiki/James_Moffat_(author) "James Moffat (author)") novel *Suedehead*.[[3]](#cite_note-Morrissey_Gets_It_Off_His_Chest-3)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) In the late 1970s, a suedehead revival developed following the 1977 skinhead revival. This originated with a small number of individuals such as [Hoxton Tom McCourt](/wiki/Hoxton_Tom_McCourt "Hoxton Tom McCourt"), who also became involved with the [mod revival](/wiki/Mod_revival "Mod revival") of the late 1970s.

In the early- to mid-1970s, many suedeheads also owned [Lambretta](/wiki/Lambretta "Lambretta") scooters and there was a type of mini-[mod](/wiki/Mod_(subculture) "Mod (subculture)") revival. It was a crossover movement based on style, music, clothes and was most common amongst working-class teenagers living in the larger inner-city conurbations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 1980s, a number of [fanzines](/wiki/Fanzines "Fanzines") were dedicated to the Suedehead culture. This includes *The Suedehead Times*, which released three issues between 1985 and 1986.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Morrissey released a single called "[Suedehead](/wiki/Suedehead "Suedehead")" in 1988, although the lyrics appear to have nothing to do with suedehead subculture.
